title: Customer Concentration: The Exit Discount Most Business Owners Don't See Coming
linkedin:
Your biggest client just renewed. That should feel like the right way to start a new financial year.

For a future buyer, it reads differently.

The threshold where buyers start adjusting offers for customer concentration risk is when one client accounts for more than 20 per cent of revenue. At 30 per cent, it often shapes the deal structure itself, not just the multiple.

The discount does not always show up as a lower headline price. It shows up in how the deal is written. An earnout makes part of the proceeds conditional on the anchor client staying for one to two years post-settlement. A condition precedent requires written confirmation from that client before the sale closes. Both mechanisms transfer concentration risk from the buyer to the seller.

The arithmetic matters. A business normalising at $600,000 EBITDA might attract 5x with a well-distributed client base, and 3.5x with significant concentration at the anchor level. That is a $900,000 difference in enterprise value for identical underlying earnings. That gap does not close in negotiation. It closes in the years before a sale process starts.

The fix is not to exit the anchor relationship. It is to build alongside it. Twelve to twenty-four months of growing a second and third client tier, until no single relationship dominates revenue, changes how a buyer reads the same business entirely.

With the new financial year five days away, understanding where your own client mix sits, and what it says to a future buyer, is the most useful exercise available right now.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog. Link in the first comment.

If one client accounts for 30 per cent of your revenue, that relationship feels essential. To a future buyer, it reads as concentration risk.

The discount usually does not appear as a lower headline price. It shows up in deal structure: an earnout tied to whether the anchor client stays post-settlement, or a condition requiring written confirmation before the sale closes.

With June 30 five days away, it is worth a quick look at your own revenue mix. How distributed is it really, and what would a buyer see if they opened that report?

google:
For business owners on the east coast who are approaching a sale or thinking about one in the next few years, customer concentration is one of the risks that most consistently produces a gap between what an owner expects to receive and what a buyer will actually offer.

The threshold where buyers begin adjusting is when one customer accounts for more than 20 per cent of revenue. At that point, the buyer's model asks what the earnings look like if that relationship changes after settlement. The adjustment shows up not always as a lower headline price but in how the deal is structured: earnout clauses, conditions tied to the anchor client, or a lower earnings multiple applied to the whole business.

The most effective response is building the second and third client tier over twelve to twenty-four months before a planned sale, until no single relationship accounts for a disproportionate share. The anchor client does not need to leave. The portfolio simply needs to grow around it.

The new financial year begins on Monday. ProfitPulse helps business owners across Queensland, New South Wales, and Victoria understand where their exit value sits today and what would lift it before a sale. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
